item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Votes don't help the author, so I've vowed to always leave a comment with mine, at the very least whenever I rate an article negatively. If you feel the same, help out by leaving critique with more of your votes. Comment follows:

It took a while for to work out that the recording is different each time. I'd assumed that the anomaly was just that the aired version was different from the original, or something (and even then only because 'the recording changes' had been done so many times before and I hoped this would be different), and only challenged that after wondering "why run multiple tests?" It'd be nice to see that made a little clearer at the start. I assume the number was chosen for its similarity to 1733.

I finished the article without really knowing what I was supposed to take away from it. There's a few components that are presumably connected but feel completely disparate to me. I wanted to know more about the situation and DJ Slimy, but there's also other stuff that feels rammed in there: the black screens, the way everything gets reset and the characters are either compelled or cool with it, the rabbit skull. I'd like to follow a single thread to its conclusion, or at least get close to it and have it wrested away. I'm not left with a clear enough question to ask, just a vague sentiment of "something's going on here".

No single element of this feels original, which would be fine if their combination felt fresh, but to me it doesn't feel like a combination at all. You caught my interest and my curiosity in Delgado's interview, so points for that. Sadly there's more that I don't like here than that which I do, so it's a -1 overall.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Small typo? Ferraro turns into Ferrera

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: What's with the crosslink to 4911?

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I've been thinking about this comment and functionally that's one of the points.

Sometimes there are mysteries that defy answers.

item.title: an update
item.content:encoded: SCP-3566 is such a natural crosslink

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Haven't felt so disjointed after reading an SCP in a long time. +1

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I've re-read this a couple times now and it's really grown on me. The components of the anomaly all feel like well trodden ground, but the execution makes it a good read anyway, which gives the imagery a chance to sink in. It's the imagery that's makes this - redbone at 120 decibels, Parsons and the rabbit skull, the flashing words, Delgado's nonsensical hostility - I can't tell if it works despite the incoherence or because of it. Either way, this has stuck with me like a vaguely-remembered nightmare, which is my ideal 10/10 perfect thing to come away with after reading an SCP.

Also Donald Glover is a pop culture genius and Redbone will never be the same for me again.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I can't decide what I think of this. I was really into the set up, but I don't know that this delivers. A lot of the elements seemed near disjointed, and I didn't get a strong sense of internal logic.

In my opinion, the initial interview was the strongest point — it was all downhill from there, a mess of unresolved and unsatifying story threads. While that's fine in some cases, I couldn't even really imagine the answer to some of the mysteries you set up, and the amount of threads and players turned me off this a lot.

No vote.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
This was nightmarish. Good job!

I'll just ignore that itch on the side of my neck…

item.title: Baxranjo
item.content:encoded: I love it when Scips are able to take joke-sounding concepts and make them real. The idea of a TBBT SCP should lead to silliness, but this is quite good horror. I did get SCP-2960 and SCP-1733 vibes, but that's no detraction. +1

item.title: Re: HONEY, WHERE ARE MY PAAAAAANTS?
item.content:encoded:
In the immortal words of Alex Trebek:

"You have lived a terrifying life."

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Coda is good.

I feel that not exploring the implications of a guy that retains knowledge of how to memetically infect people is a wasted opportunity though.

I respect the opinion to leave it as subtext however. It's hard to put well known IP in this format and still be creepy.

EDIT: I agree that the dialogue in the test log is fantastic. I have a hard time with that so well done.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
It's definitely not low priority, and in fact the coda should make it clear that they're taking it seriously.

There's rodent imagery laced throughout the rest of the article, but subtle.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Well I'm creeped the fuck out.

+1

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Also my thoughts. There were a lot of interesting things in this, but whatever theme there is holding it together (if such a thing was intended) is too obscure for me.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I…but what…holy shit, what did I read?

This is a really great example of wtf in text form, and really entertaining throughout.

The dialogue is really good between the various characters. Completely believable.

Emphatic +1

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I just don't get this at all I'm afraid. It just feels very random.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I lose immersion regarding POI-701, the real life Delgado. They obviously know more than the Foundation does, but we're just cool with him not co-operating?

The neck scratching infecting the agent is legit creepy and builds tension nicely for later on, but it kicks me completely out of the narrative when the Foundation is like whatev, low priority. Dude infected an agent and not even an amnestic? Not logical to me.

You manage a great climax although the rabbit skull theme seems very random and unconnected. is it something with the show?

EDIT: No vote for now

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Even if it wasn’t the authors’ intent, this still ultimately comes off as just parroting common criticisms while adding in a few horror elements that don’t really get contextualized. I think the base idea that spawned this piece is a cool one, but I’m personally not a fan of this particular angle and execution.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Notably, though he appeared in SCP-3441 age 9, Armitage would have been less than one year old at the time of filming.

Wrong number?

item.title: Re: -1
item.content:encoded: Color me surprised. Regardless, it comes across that way despite your actual opinions. And my objection to the pause of Foundation clinicality remains. The Foundation isn't a TV review blog.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I've done a few revisions to clear up what happened to Agent Ferrera, and to distance it from 1733 just a little.

item.title: Re: -1
item.content:encoded: As am I. As such, this could be about any less-popular network sitcom. We just thought that one was an easier touchstone.

item.title: Re: HONEY, WHERE ARE MY PAAAAAANTS?
item.content:encoded:
This is based on a true story about the time I went to watch a CBS sitcom taping for a horrible show called 9JKL. I was stuck in the soundstage bleachers for 9 hours with an awful warm-up comedian as they blared loud music every ten minutes, made us dance, and otherwise do other things between takes so they could milk as much laughter out of us as possible.

SCPs are real.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Upvoted at "We're all dead here, bazinga boy."

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I agree that the description comes off as mouthpiecing (granted, I know nothing about BBT myself that isn't other people complaining about it), but this just gets so fucking creepy by the end, I couldn't not like it. It's like, the basketball DVD, but with a smaller cast. Neck itching, geez.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I agree with you - while I'm generally non-prescriptivist IRL, I feel Foundation stuff always works best when it's as precise and correct as possible. Fixing now.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
It would in my opinion, which is all that's relevant to this thread. My original comment was on an issue that, for me, would absolutely improve the article. Perhaps not enough for everyone, perhaps not even enough for me on its own, but certainly an improvement. I see it as no different at all from pointing out then/than, affect/effect, or any other grammatical errors.

I'll also note I've not voted in either direction, so it's not as though I've impeded the article's existence.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Aaaaaaand smaller, 10% dimensions size image has been implemented!

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Itch vs. Scratch.

I don't like this blatant prescriptivism. Language is both flexible and fluid, and as your article points out, "People often use itch and scratch in place of each other in everyday speech". Yes, this is supposed to be scientific document, yes this SCP has a few hiccups in clinical tone. That doesn't mean that being pedantic over such non-technical and minor words is actually going to improve the work.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Sorry, didn't realise! I'd appreciate you doing that, thanks

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Itch vs. Scratch. These are not the same, yet you used them interchangibly throughout.

Examples
Hide

(Delgado scratches behind his ear, looking at the ground.)

This is correct.

(Delgado begins itching a point his neck)

On his neck would fix the sentence as a whole, but this is still incorrect; unless Delgado is telling you that he has an itch, he's scratching his neck.

Parsons now randomly will itch his neck a number of times throughout the episode.
Now, rarely, single-frame black screens bearing the word RABBIT in dark blue text will appear, at which point Parsons will repeatedly scratch at a point on his throat until he is able to tear a small hole in the skin.

Hide

I have several other issues with this, mostly echoing what's already been noted above, but felt obliged to include this nugget as it seems no one else had yet.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Agreed. Rimple, do you mind if I resize that? It's murder on mobile devices with limited data plans.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
This feels like a collage of typical horror elements, most of them ill-defined, resulting in a piece that feels like it's treading common ground while being thematically lost. There's a whole lot of loose ends and ill-defined characters, with the strong point probably being the first interview. The neck-scratching initially seemed interesting enough, particularly since one of the Foundation guys was affected by the end… and that's it. The implications of this aren't addressed at all. Does the agent share the same fate as Sheldon at the end? If so, what does that mean? Why should I care?

I can't fault the pacing and it did generally hold my interest until the end. There's also a lot of good imagery here, and the central premise is… ok, I guess? At least from what I gathered. At any rate, I understand some of the choices you made here, but ultimately I've seen this sort of thing on the site enough times that this doesn't work for me in its current state.

item.title: Re: -1
item.content:encoded: Because everything an author writes in their stuff is 100% what they firmly believe, right?

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Does the image need to be 2.7 MB? It takes like a minute to load.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Dislike how the internal logic feels artificial, and hoping you do have an idea or plan for what's going on. Originally I didn't like how it felt like a mouthpiece, but then I started to realize who were the "bad guys." On the horror, great job. Really disturbing. This is great in many areas but very weak in a couple imo. +1 despite the weaknesses.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Fantastically surreal with the apparent internal logic to keep this from turning into random mishmash. Nicely done.

+1

item.title: Re: -1
item.content:encoded: I am a fan of The Big Bang Theory, and I wrote the majority of the description.

item.title: Re: -1
item.content:encoded: Seconded; all I came away with here is "the author strongly dislikes this show"

Mmm. I really, really dislike SCPs that are just blatant author mouthpieces. Half the initial description reads more like one of the authors speaking their own opinions on the show (which I haven't watched more than a few minutes of), than anything the Foundation would conceivably note.

Maybe I'm just super numb to horror at this point, but the logs felt like you combined the weakest aspects of 1981 and 1733 (the red light, the mysterious words, the unimpeding mutilation). All I got was that there's an evil DJ Slimy guy? Nothing else gave any clues.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I don't know whether to be creeped out or to laugh at this but you get a +1 from me, my man

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
There's clearly something extremely unsettling going on, but hell if I know what it is.

+1 for creeping me out as well as for calling TBBT out on its shit.

item.title: HONEY, WHERE ARE MY PAAAAAANTS?
item.content:encoded:
I legit have no idea what to say about this. Stonefish shared a story on Discord a while back and I was like "that needs to be a skip" and then, you know.

Thanks to PeppersGhost for some good helpful feedback on his
